coredata.dc:title: Self-co-reactant and ion-annihilation electrogenerated chemiluminescence of carbon nanodots
coredata.dc:creator: Long, Yan-Min
Bao, Lei
Peng, Ying
Zhang, Zhi-Ling
Pang, Dai-Wen
coredata.dc:description: Recently, the electrogenerated chemiluminescence (ECL) of carbon nanodots (C-dots) have been detected and the signal is applied for sensitive determination of environmental pollutes and biological active molecules. A few of researches have been reported on the mechanisms of C-dots that the electro-generated cation and anion intermediates undergo electron-transfer annihilation to give off strong ECL emission, which is quite similar to other nanoparticles. In this work, the ECL of C-dots through both co-reactant and annihilation pathways was examined by polarizing the working and counter electrodes in each isolated compartment of an H-shaped electrolytic cell. The peculiar self-co-reacted anodic ECL of C-dots was observed for the first time, to our best knowledge. The oxygen-containing groups especially the benzylic alcohol on the surface were proposed to turn into a strong reductant after losing one electron and one proton on anode, reducing the electrogenerated C-dots+ to exciting state C-dots* and causing the ECL emission. The ion-annihilation ECL under potential cycling and stepping was also observed which is mixed with co-reactant ECL. This work provided an insight into the distinctive structures of C-dots and the corresponding luminescent peculiarity.

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Anodic ECL emission of C-dots without external co-reactants
Self-co-reactant ECL and the related mechanism
The ion-annihilation mixed with self-co-reactant ECL of C-dots when pulsing between negative and positive regions
The emission spectra and surface energy state responsible for ECL

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: National High Technology Research and Development Program of China
National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: We thank Professor Shengli Chen from Wuhan University for kindly offering instructive advice on this work. This work is jointly supported by National Natural Science Foundation of China ( 21535005 , 21375100 , 21677063 ), the National High Technology Research and Development Program of China (863 Program, no. 2013AA032204 ), the 111 Project (111-2-10), and RMIT Vice-Chancellor's Postdoctoral Fellowship.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The configuration for ECL determination (left side) and the respective signals (right side) as indicated. The imposed potential pulse applied to working electrode from 0 to −1.5 V or +1.5 V was curved correspondingly (blue dash line) on the right panel. The electrolyte solution contained 0.2 mol/L pH7.2 phosphate buffer and 0.2 mg/mL C-dots. Three-electrode system consisted of platinum disk, platinum wire and Ag/AgCl as working (WE), counter (CE) and reference (RE) electrodes, respectively. Scan rate: 100 mV/s. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

(a) Cyclic voltammograph and corresponding ECL signal of 0.2 mg/mL C-dots in 0.2 mol/L pH7.2 phosphate buffer. PMT bias voltage: 700 V. Effect of (b) 2000 U/mL superoxide dismutase, (c) 1 mmol/L xanthine mixed with 0.5 U/mL xanthine oxidase, and (d) 0.1 mol/L hydrogen peroxide on the anodic ECL of C-dots. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)
Effects of (a) annealing treatment under gas flow of H2/Ar and (b) solution pH on the PL and ECL intensity as indicated. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

ECL transients in 0.2 mol/L pH7.2 phosphate buffer containing 0.1 mg/mL C-dots obtained by (a) potential stepping between +1.5 V for 1s and −1.2 V for 1s (vs. Ag/AgCl) and (b) potentials stepping between +1.5 V for 1s and −1.2 V for 3s (as shown in blue curves. Bias voltage: 600 V). The ECL curves of 0.1 mg/mL C-dots in 0.2 mol/L pH7.2 phosphate buffer when the electrode potential was cycled between −1.5 and +1.5 V at the scan rate of 100 mV/s (c) and 500 mV/s (d), respectively. Bias voltage: 700 V. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)
(a) The ECL spectrum and (b) the proposed transition of ECL and PL of C-dots in pH7.2 phosphate buffer.
The proposed mechanism of self-co-reactant anodic ECL of C-dots.
The proposed mechanism of ion annihilation ECL of C-dots.

head.ce:title: Self-co-reactant and ion-annihilation electrogenerated chemiluminescence of carbon nanodots

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Analytical Chemistry for Biology and Medicine (Ministry of Education), College of Chemistry and Molecular Sciences, State Key Laboratory of Virology, The Institute for Advanced Studies, and Wuhan Institute of Biotechnology, Wuhan University, Wuhan 430072, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Analytical Chemistry for Biology and Medicine (Ministry of Education)
College of Chemistry and Molecular Sciences
The Institute for Advanced Studies, and Wuhan Institute of Biotechnology

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institution of Environment and Health, Jianghan University, Wuhan 430056, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Engineering, RMIT University, Melbourne 3001, Australia

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: The authors contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Recently, the electrogenerated chemiluminescence (ECL) of carbon nanodots (C-dots) have been detected and the signal is applied for sensitive determination of environmental pollutes and biological active molecules. A few of researches have been reported on the mechanisms of C-dots that the electro-generated cation and anion intermediates undergo electron-transfer annihilation to give off strong ECL emission, which is quite similar to other nanoparticles. In this work, the ECL of C-dots through both co-reactant and annihilation pathways was examined by polarizing the working and counter electrodes in each isolated compartment of an H-shaped electrolytic cell. The peculiar self-co-reacted anodic ECL of C-dots was observed for the first time, to our best knowledge. The oxygen-containing groups especially the benzylic alcohol on the surface were proposed to turn into a strong reductant after losing one electron and one proton on anode, reducing the electrogenerated C-dots+ to exciting state C-dots* and causing the ECL emission. The ion-annihilation ECL under potential cycling and stepping was also observed which is mixed with co-reactant ECL. This work provided an insight into the distinctive structures of C-dots and the corresponding luminescent peculiarity.

ce:section.ce:para: Fluorescent nanoparticles have been popular in diverse fields for their superior optical properties (e.g. size- and composition-tunable emissions), high surface area and ease of biofunctionalization [1–3]. Since the electrogenerated chemiluminescence (ECL) of Si nanocrystals reported by Bard et al. in both annihilation and co-reactant manners in 2002 [4], almost typical semiconductor quantum dots (e.g. CdSe [5], CdTe [6], PbS [7], and core-shell structured CdSe/ZnSe [8]) have been included in the ECL study. The mechanisms of ECL from these nanoparticles have been well documented [5,8,9] as well as the related bioanalysis [10–14]. Recently, a new kind of fluorescent nanoparticles–carbon nanodots (C-dots) has quickly attracted attentions, due to their prominent emission merits, excellent biocompatibility and low toxicity [15–20]. In 2009, Chi et al. observed the ECL from C-dots in aqueous solution and officially brought them as an emitter into the ECL realm [21]. Subsequently, the applications in sensitive detection of environmental pollutes and neurotransmitter were reported based on ECL signals of C-dots [22,23]. Moreover, ECL is employed to probe the surface energy levels and corresponding structures of C-dots, due to its susceptibility to the surface energy states of nanoparticles [21,24].

The aqueous ECL from C-dots has been firstly detected by cycling the electrode potential between +1.8 V and −1.5 V (vs. Ag/AgCl) in pH7.0 PBS buffer [21]. A strong cathodic emission together with a weak anodic emission has been observed during one cycle on the platinum working electrode. Similar to the quantum dots [5,8,9,25,26], the ECL mechanism of electron-transfer annihilation between electro-generated C-dots+ and C-dots− has been proposed. In presence of co-reactant K2S2O8, the co-reactant ECL of C-dots was also observed by scanning potential in the negative range. In this way, the oxidation of intermediate C-dots− by SO4 −· radical resulted in strong cathodic ECL. As discussed, the mechanism has been put forward on taking C-dots as other zero-dimensional nanomaterials like QDs or metal nanoparticles without considering its unique surface structure. Some important questions have not been resolved, for example, which specific structures of C-dots go through the electron transfer during potential polarization to form the charged intermediate? Which structure-related surface energy trap is responsible for the exciton recombination and the resultant ECL? Answers to those questions are essential for deeply understanding the photics of C-dots and their extensive applications.

C-dots were usually less than 10 nm in size, and consist of nano-sized graphite core and diverse oxygen-containing groups on the surface [15–17]. The rich surface functional groups shape energy traps on the particle and significantly affect electron transition routes. It is well accepted that oxygen-containing groups including carboxyl, carbonyl, alcohol, phenol, quinones, etc. are active in redox chemical reactions. Some of them, such as benzylic alcohol, can become relatively stable and highly active reductant after electro-oxidation, co-reacting Ru(bpy)3 2+ to emit anodic ECL [27]. Moreover, it has been reported that the fluorescence of C-dots can be quenched through donating or accepting the photo-induced electrons with external redox chemicals [28,29], which implied that C-dots were redox agents under external excitation. The above results suggested that under the electrochemical driving force, ECL rising from some peculiarity of C-dots such as surface oxygen-containing groups would be demonstrated.

In this work, the ECL of C-dots was investigated in an H-shaped electrolytic cell, with which the signal of corresponding electrode was separately collected. It was found that besides ionic annihilation of ECL, C-dots can emit light in a pathway so called ‘self-co-reactant’ mechanism. In such pathway, the benzylic alcohol on the surface were proposed to turn into a strong reductant after losing one electron and one proton in a positive potential apply. Such reductant can reduce the electrogenerated C-dots+ to exciting state C-dots*, causing the ECL light emission. This self-co-reactant ECL process combined with ion-annihilation ECL process contributes to the overall ECL signals under potential cycling and stepping from positive to negative potentials. This work provided an insight into the distinctive structures and luminescent peculiarities of C-dots.

Carbon fibers were obtained from Shanghai Tansu Manufactory. All other analytical-grade reagents were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. The ultrapure water was prepared by an ultrapure water system of Millipore.

Briefly, C-dots were obtained by collecting the fraction of nominal molecular weight less than 3 kDa via ultrafiltration of the electro-oxidized products of carbon fibers in 0.1 mol L−1 NaH2PO4 solution. The morphology and surface group information of C-dots were characterized with transmission electron microscopy and fourier transform infrared spectroscopy, respectively. The photoluminescence spectrum of C-dots was obtained under the excitation of 330 nm on a Nanolog-typed fluorescence spectrometer of Horiba Instruments.

The electrochemical testing system included a three-electrode configuration of an Ag/AgCl reference electrode, a platinum disk working electrode and platinum wire counter electrode in a H-shaped electrolytic cell and the electrolyte solution of phosphate buffer. The measurements were carried out on a MPI-E II electrochemiluminescence analytical system (Xi'an Remex Analytical Instrument) through polarizing electrodes in the fashion of cyclic voltammetry or potential step. At the same time, the ECL signals were recorded at the bias voltage of 600–800 V. The ECL spectrum was plotted by fitting a series of ECL data at the potential of +1.5 V (vs. Ag/AgCl) when cutting off the signal with long-pass filters ranging from 455 nm to 780 nm.
The lyophilized C-dots powder was heated at 300 °C under the continuous gas flow of H2/Ar in the tubular furnace for one hour.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Anodic ECL emission of C-dots without external co-reactants
ce:section.ce:para: The C-dots were prepared exactly according to the recent work of our group [27]. As shown in Fig. S1, the C-dots were monodispersive with the size distribution of 2.7 ± 0.4 nm (Fig. S1a). The FT-IR results in Fig. S1b exhibited the absorption bands of νO-H at ∼3430 cm−1, νCO at ∼1720 cm−1, δO-H at ∼1360 cm−1and νC-O at ∼1170 cm−1, which indicated the oxygen-containing groups such as alcohol, phenol, carbonyl and carboxyl, etc. capped the C-dots surface. With the excitation of 330 nm, C-dots emitted photoluminescence maximized at 449 nm (Fig. S1c). The suspension of C-dots in water exhibited blue-colored fluorescence under UV excitation (Fig. S1c, inset). With inspect of ECL signals in previous reports, we noticed that a strong ECL occurred even in absence of co-reactant while cycling potential from 0 to −1.5 V (vs. Ag/AgCl) in pH7.0 PBS buffer containing C-dots [21]. This result is different from other nanoparticles, such as Ge nanoparticles [9], in which more stable species produced in the negative potential range meet with the ones formed in the positive potential region to generate a much stronger anodic ECL against cathodic ones. Generally, three-electrode system for ECL determination usually has been set in one compartment for convenience and it is hard to discern whether the signals are from working electrode or counter electrode. Such uncertainty would cause misunderstanding of the mechanisms. Herein, the ECL measurements of C-dots were carried out in an ‘H-shaped’ electrolytic cell with a Pt disk working electrode and Ag/AgCl reference electrode separated from the counter electrode by a salt bridge as sketched in Fig. 1 . In this way, the signal from working electrode or counter electrode can be separately collected, which facilitates to figure out the origin of ECL and related mechanisms. When potential scanned during 0 and -1.5 V, three electrodes together gave off a strong ECL emission (the top in Fig. 1), while almost no emission was detected on the working electrode alone (the middle in Fig. 1). The results suggested an anodic ECL emission, since counter electrode was correspondingly positively polarized as potential was negatively polarized on working electrode from 0 to −1.5 V. This speculation was confirmed by the strong ECL emission upon working electrode positively polarized from 0 to +1.5 V (the bottom in Fig. 1). Moreover, the intensity of ECL on counter electrode was almost equal to that on three electrodes together (Fig. S2), pronouncing the ECL signals are solely from anodic ECL of C-dots. Notably, C-dots can give off an anodic ECL emission upon positive scanning even in the absence of additional co-reactants.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Self-co-reactant ECL and the related mechanism
ce:section.ce:para: To figure out the underlying mechanism of the anodic ECL, the electrochemistry and ECL response of C-dots were synchronously recorded when the working electrode potential was cycling from 0 to +1.5 V in 0.2 mol/L pH7.2 PBS buffer containing 0.2 mg/mL C-dots. As demonstrated in Fig. 2 a, the anodic current rose from the potential of +0.7 V and maximized at +1.5 V (0.73 mA), and the ECL photocurrent onset at +0.48 V and maximized at +1.5 V (115 a.u.) at the same time. However, the dependency of ECL on electrochemistry of C-dots was hardly obtained from the results, because the significant current of oxygen evolution almost occupied the whole voltammographic window. According to the previous reports, C-dots could be electro-oxidized to the charged intermediate of C-dots+ and transformed into excited state C-dots∗ in the presence of anodic co-reactant [21]. The continously rising ECL intensities as potential positively polarizing in this work indicated that more and more C-dots+ generated by hole (h+) injection on anode. In addition, dissolved oxygen and reductive intermediates evolved from water splitting in the range of 0 ∼ +1.5 V may work as the co-reactant candidates for anodic ECL of C-dots. As for oxidation process of water, the possible intermediates involved have been put forward, even if the actual mechanism was unclear now. The process could irreversibly produce oxygen radical (O2 −) and H2O2 [30], which are possible candidates acting as co-reactants for the ECL of C-dots. Firstly, the identical ECL under air, Ar and O2 saturated atmosphere (Fig. S3) revealed that O2 molecules had little impact on the ECL process. Secondly, the O2 − intermediate, provided with adequate reducing ability, was reported to be the co-reactant of anodic ECL from quantum dots [13]. Superoxide dismutase (SOD), an important anti-oxidation reagent, can efficiently catalyze the dismutation of O2 − into O2 and H2O, and suppressing the activity of O2 − [31]. Besides, O2 can be reduced to O2 − in presence of xanthine and xanthine oxidase [32,33]. Then whether O2 − involved in ECL process was explored by adding these two kinds of reagents into the solution. The ECL signals showed no difference between before and after adding SOD or mixture of xanthine and xanthine oxidase (Fig. 2b and c), suggesting that the intermediate O2 − did not take part in the anodic ECL process of C-dots either. Again, the addition of H2O2 exhibited little effect on the anodic ECL (Fig. 2d), suggesting that H2O2 was not involved in the anodic ECL process of C-dots as well.

The oxygen reduction and water oxidation were excluded to contribute to the anodic ECL, and the distance between electrodes in H-shaped cell is too far for generated C-dot+ diffusing into the cathodic part and meeting with C-dot− in such a short time. Our recent work uncovered that C-dots can act as the co-reactant for anodic ECL of Ru(bpy)3 2+ during potential scanning in the range of +0.2 ∼ +1.4 V (vs.Ag/AgCl) [27], in which the benzylic alcohol on C-dots surface transferred into the strong reductant of α-hydroxybenzylic radical (C-dots (C6H5C
HOH), E0 ([C6H5CHOH]
/C6H5CHO) = −1.25 V vs. NHE) after successively losing one electron and one proton on electrode. The reductive radical intermediate reduced electro-generated Ru(bpy)3 3+ to the excited state Ru(bpy)3 2+∗ and caused an anodic ECL emission of Ru(bpy)3 2+. On the other, the previous study on the electrochemistry of C-dots in organic electrolyte revealed that the irreversible oxidation of C-dots occurred at +1.5 V (vs. Ag QRE) which is equal to +2.30 V (vs. NHE). Together with the blue-colored PL emission of C-dots (λem = 449 nm, 2.76 eV), the standard potential of C-dots+ /C-dots* pair is calcualted to be −0.46 V. This theoretical result indicated that the intermediate of α-hydroxybenzylic radical is capable of reducing the C-dots+ to the excited species of C-dots*, causing anodic ECL emission. Thus, benzylic alcohol on C-dots can facilitate anodic ECL of C-dots. Herein, we examined the effect of annealing treatment and pH value on the anodic ECL. As shown in Fig. 3 a, after annealed by 10% H2, the anodic ECL of C-dots almost disappeared, which is consitent with our previous work [27]. The reductive annealing under H2 deoxygenated the benzylic alcohol units on C-dots, which was confirmed by a significant decrease on the IR absorption of OH at ∼3430 and ∼1360 cm−1 (Fig. S1b), resulting in a decreased -anodic ECL of C-dots. In Fig. 3b, the dependence of photoluminescence (PL) intensity against pH exhibited a “volcano” shape, showing that the intensity increased with the pH ranging from 1.5 to 4.7 and declined from 5.8 to 10.3. While the ECL intensity continuously increased as pH increased. In comparison of PL and ECL, the exciton transitions are similar, but the difference in the course of each is the electron injection route [25,26]. The disparity between the pH dependency of PL and ECL was possibly attributed to the different effects of pH on electron injection or stability of the intermediates. It was reported that higher pH facilitated the deprotonation of benzylic alcohol on C-dots to transform into reductive α-hydroxybenzylic radical thus enhancing ECL emission [27]. In this term, the pH correlation with anodic ECL of C-dots further supported the key role of benzylic alcohol unit of C-dots. Accordingly, we propose a so called 'self-co-reactant' ECL route for the anodic ECL of C-dots as shown in Scheme 1 . During potential cycling in positive range, C-dots was electro-oxidized into cation radical of C-dots+ , while the benzylic alcohol on surface transformed into strong reductive intermediate of α-hydroxybenzylic radical after one-electron oxidization followed by deprotonation. The electron-transfer recombination of the exciton pair (C-dots+ -(C6H5C
HOH)) produced the anodic ECL emission.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The ion-annihilation mixed with self-co-reactant ECL of C-dots when pulsing between negative and positive regions
ce:section.ce:para:
In this work, the ion annihilation ECL of C-dots was studied both in potential stepping and cycling manners. As shown in Fig. 4 a, strong ECL signals were detected by stepping potential between +1.5 V and −1.2 V. Notably, a sharp increasing of ECL signals appeared when initially stepping potential was at +1.5 V from 0 to 0.25s. At this stage, only C-dots+ were generated. As discussed above, we consider such signal was highly correlated with the anodic ECL of self-co-reactant C-dots, where C6H5C
HOH formed on the surface of C-dots acts as co-reactants to C-dots+ . Moreover, after the intensity reached to 28 (a.u), the ECL signal underwent a slow decay to 20 (a.u) from 0.25 s to 1s. Such type of slow decay is often seen in the co-reactant ECL [4], which distinguishes the rapid decay from the anion-cation annihilation ECL [5].

Then from 1s to 2s, the potential were stepped at −1.2 V to form C-dots- . Therefore, from the next second (3s), as the potential stepped back to +1.5 V, the accumulated C-dots- collided with generated C-dots+ , resulting in a strong and sharp peak (i). As time elapsed, a broad and weak peak (ii) revealed, which showed a quite similarity to the ECL signal observed at the duration of 0-1s. Thus this second peak (ii) is attributed to self-co-reactant ECL process as proposed. For the further investigation, the stepping time at −1.2 V was increased from 1s to 3s and remained stepping at +1.5 V for 1s (Fig. 4b). It clearly showed that the intensity of sharp ECL portion (i) enhanced as much as two times with more C-dots− accumulated along the prolonged cathodic stepping for 3s, while the ECL from self-co-reactant process (ii) remained the same. This also verified that the ECL of C-dots on stepping between −1.2 V and +1.5 V underwent both anion-cation annihilated ECL and self-co-reactant ECL process.

The similar ECL behaviors are also observed by cycling the potential between - 1.5 V and +1.5 V. The ECL in Fig. 4c showed the following features: (1) no signal could be detected at the first scan in cathodic potential region and a weak signal showed in the returning scan in cathodic potential region after the anodic scan, indicating that the cathodic ECL purely originated from annihilation between anions and cations; (2) the anodic ECL signal was much stronger than the cathodic signal, implying that the anodic ECL included ECL emissions both from co-reactant and annihilation process. The intensities of anodic and cathodic ECL enhanced as scan rate increased, and the ECL started earlier at the rate of 500 mV/s than that at 100 mV/s, which was similar to the behavior of semiconductor quantum dots [5]. The results could be explained that more cations and anions could survive in cyclic scan at higher scan rate and transfer electrons to cause the stronger and earlier ECL emissions. The ECL profile in Fig. 4c and d showed different onset potential and intensity compared to that in Figs. 2 and 3. That difference is becuase both self-co-reactant and ion-annihilation ECL occured as the potential scanning from −1.5 to +1.5 V in Fig. 4c and d. The anion radical C-dots− produced in negatively potential scan would consume portion of cation radical C-dots+ , causing decrease of anodic ECL signal in Fig. 4c and d in comparison of Figs. 2 and 3.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The emission spectra and surface energy state responsible for ECL
ce:section.ce:para: As shown in Fig. 5 a, the ECL spectrum peaked at 530 nm, and the band gaps correlated to ECL and PL (λmax = 449 nm) are 2.34 eV and 2.76 eV, repectively. The difference between those energy gaps of 0.42 eV suggested different energy levels accounting for them, which is similar to the previous reports [21,34]. ECL involves the electron transfer between nanoparticles and electrode or co-reactants, which includes electron passage through the surface of nanoparticles. In this course, the electron could be trapped by the surface states [35] and the radiative recombination of surface trapped electron-hole pairs to result in ECL emissions. In other words, the ECL was susceptible to the surface states of nanoparticles as compared with PL, which is sensitive to the core of nanoparticles [35]. Accordingly, the ECL emission of C-dots could also be attributed to the surface states as that of silicon nanoparticles [4] and most semiconductor quantum dots were [25,26] (Fig. 5b).

The ECL emissions of C-dots in two pathways have been comprehensively examined in this work. To our best knowledge, the self-co-reactant anodic ECL of C-dots has been revealed for the first time. The benzylic alcohol on the surface transformed into reductive co-reactant after losing one electron and one proton on the anode. The electron transfer from α-hydroxybenzylic radical to C-dots+ formed the excited state C-dots*, allowing to emit the self-co-reactant anodic ECL. In this case, the energy level for the electron of exiton pair during the anodic run was uncovered to be the surface energy trap corresponding to the α-hydroxybenzylic radical with the value of E0 = −1.25 V vs. NHE. While the energy level for the hole of exiton pair needs more in-detailed investigation to be clarified. Moreover, the annihilation ECL, proposed to be a mixed signal of annihilation and co-reactant ECL emissions was revealed from both potential stepping and cycling modes. The big red shift between spectra of ECL and PL demonstrated that ECL originated from the surface states of C-dots. This work provided an insight into the ECL process of C-dots with two pathways, which offered a theoretical fundamental for optimizing the ECL system with C-dots as emitters.
The authors report no conflicts of interest in this work.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We thank Professor Shengli Chen from Wuhan University for kindly offering instructive advice on this work. This work is jointly supported by National Natural Science Foundation of China (21535005, 21375100, 21677063), the National High Technology Research and Development Program of China (863 Program, no. 2013AA032204), the 111 Project (111-2-10), and RMIT Vice-Chancellor's Postdoctoral Fellowship.

ce:section.ce:para:
The following is the supplementary data related to this article:

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data related to this article can be found at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.carbon.2017.11.073.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Semiconductor clusters, nanocrystals and quantum dots
Perspectives on the physical chemistry of semiconductor nanocrystals
Semiconductor nanocrystals: structure, properties, and band gap engineering
Electrochemistry and electrogenerated chemiluminescence from silicon nanocrystal quantum dots
Electrochemistry and electrogenerated chemiluminescence of CdTe nanoparticles
Electrogenerated chemiluminescence from PbS quantum dots
Effect of surface passivation on the electrogenerated chemiluminescence of CdSe/ZnSe nanocrystals
Cytosensing and dynamic monitoring of cell surface carbohydrate expression by electrochemiluminescence of quantum dots
Enhanced electrochemiluminescence of CdSe quantum dots composited with CNTs and PDDA for sensitive immunoassay
Fundamentals and bioanalytical applications of functional quantum dots as electrogenerated emitters of chemiluminescence
Anodic electrochemiluminescence of CdTe quantum dots and its energy transfer for detection of catechol derivatives
Quantum dots based electrochemiluminescent immunosensor by coupling enzymatic amplification with self-produced coreactant from oxygen reduction
Group IV nanoparticles: synthesis, properties, and biological applications
Analytical and bioanalytical applications of carbon dots
Direct demonstration of photoluminescence originated from surface functional groups in carbon nanodots
Highly efficient fluorescence switching of carbon nanodots by CO2
Uniform luminescent carbon nanodots prepared by rapid pyrolysis of organic precursors confined within nanoporous templating structures
Electrochemiluminescence of water-soluble carbon nanocrystals released electrochemically from graphite
Carbon-dot-based dual-emission nanohybrid produces a ratiometric fluorescent sensor for in vivo imaging of cellular copper ions
Peroxynitrous-acid-induced chemiluminescence of fluorescent carbon dots for nitrite sensing
Photoluminescence-tunable carbon nanodots: surface-state energy-gap tuning
Electrogenerated chemiluminescence and its biorelated applications
Revealing carbon nanodots as coreactants of the anodic electrochemiluminescence of Ru(bpy)3 2+
Photoinduced electron transfers with carbon dots
Carbon nanodots as peroxidase mimetics and their applications to glucose detection
Kinetics of oxygen evolution and dissolution on platinum electrodes
Nickel(III)-Promoted DNA cleavage with ambient dioxygen
Free radical scavenging activity of grape seed extract and antioxidants by electron spin resonance spectrometry in an H2O2/NaOH/DMSO system
Detection of superoxide anion using an isotopically labeled nitrone spin trap potential biological applications
Electrochemistry and electrochemiluminescence study of blue luminescent carbon nanocrystals
Electrochemistry and electrogenerated chemiluminescence of semiconductor nanocrystals in solutions and in films
